records.content:
By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

Describe what a database is.

Explain why databases are useful in organizations.

Distinguish between data, information, databases, and database management systems.

Compare a spreadsheet-based approach with a database-based approach.

Use a small SQLite database to store and retrieve data.

Every organization depends on data. Colleges store student records. Hospitals track patients and appointments. Retail companies manage customers, products, and orders. Manufacturers track inventory, suppliers, machines, and production schedules.

A database is a structured collection of related data. Databases help organizations store, organize, search, update, protect, and share data.

Databases matter because they allow people to answer important questions, such as:

Which students are enrolled in a course?

Which customers placed orders last month?

Which products are low in inventory?

Which employees worked on a project?

Which machines need maintenance?

In this book, we will focus mostly on relational databases and SQL, which is the language commonly used to work with relational databases.

Although people often use the words interchangeably, data and information are not exactly the same.

Data are raw facts or values.

Examples:

StudentID

FirstName

LastName

Major

1001

Maya

Johnson

IST

1002

Carlos

Rivera

Business

1003

Amina

Patel

Engineering

Information is data that has been organized or interpreted so that it is useful.

Examples:

There are 3 students in the sample.

One student is majoring in Business.

Student 1003 is Amina Patel.

A database helps turn raw data into useful information.

A database is an organized collection of related data.

The word organized is important. A random list of names is not usually enough. A useful database has structure, rules, and relationships.

For example, a college registration database might include:

Students

Courses

Instructors

Course sections

Enrollments

These are related. A student enrolls in a course section. A course section is taught by an instructor. A course belongs to a department.

A database allows these related pieces of data to be stored together in a structured way.

A Database Management System, or DBMS, is software used to create, manage, and interact with databases.

Examples of database management systems include:

SQLite

MySQL

PostgreSQL

Microsoft SQL Server

Oracle Database

The database is the stored data.The DBMS is the software that manages the database.

A DBMS helps users:

Add new data

Update existing data

Delete data

Search data

Protect data

Control who can access data

Prevent inconsistent or invalid data

Spreadsheets are useful tools, especially for small tasks. However, as data becomes larger, more connected, or shared by many users, spreadsheets can become difficult to manage.

Spreadsheet

Database

Good for small, simple datasets

Good for larger, structured datasets

Often edited manually

Often managed using rules and queries

Easy to duplicate inconsistent data

Designed to reduce unnecessary duplication

Limited control over relationships

Designed to model relationships

Not ideal for many users editing at once

Better for shared organizational systems

For example, a spreadsheet might store customer and order data in one large table. A database would usually separate customers, orders, and products into different but related tables.

Relational databases organize data into tables.

A table is made of:

Columns: categories or fields of data

Rows: individual records

Cells: individual values

Example table: Student

StudentID

FirstName

LastName

Major

1001

Maya

Johnson

IST

1002

Carlos

Rivera

Business

1003

Amina

Patel

Engineering

In database terminology:

A table is often called a relation.

A row is often called a tuple.

A column is often called an attribute.

We will use both everyday language and formal database language throughout this book.

SQL stands for Structured Query Language.

SQL is used to communicate with relational databases. SQL can be used to:

Create tables

Insert data

Retrieve data

Update data

Delete data

Control access to data

A simple SQL query looks like this:SELECT *
FROM Student;

This means: show all columns and rows from the Student table.

Imagine storing all college data in one spreadsheet:

StudentName

Major

CourseName

Instructor

Room

Maya Johnson

IST

Intro to Databases

Dr. Lee

202

Carlos Rivera

Business

Intro to Databases

Dr. Lee

202

Amina Patel

Engineering

Web Development

Dr. Gomez

118

This might work at first, but problems appear quickly:

The instructor’s name may be repeated many times.

A room change might require updates in many rows.

A student’s name could be typed differently in different places.

It becomes difficult to track which students, courses, instructors, and rooms are truly separate things.

A database helps solve these problems by organizing data into related tables.

Although this book focuses on relational databases, it is useful to know that there are several types of databases.

Type

Basic Idea

Example Use

Relational database

Data stored in related tables

Student records, sales systems

Document database

Data stored as flexible documents

Web applications, product catalogs

Graph database

Data stored as nodes and relationships

Social networks, recommendation systems

Key-value database

Data stored as key-value pairs

Caching, fast lookups

Object-oriented database

Data stored as objects

Specialized software systems

Relational databases remain widely used because they are powerful, structured, and supported by SQL.

Term

Meaning

Data

Raw facts or values

Information

Data organized into a meaningful form

Database

Organized collection of related data

DBMS

Software used to manage a database

Table

Structure that stores data in rows and columns

Row

A single record in a table

Column

A field or attribute in a table

SQL

Language used to interact with relational databases

Query

A request for data from a database

Primary key

A column or set of columns that uniquely identifies a row

Answer the following questions in your own words.

What is a database?

What is the difference between data and information?

What is a DBMS?

Why might a database be better than a spreadsheet for an organization?

What is SQL used for?

In a table, what is the difference between a row and a column?

What question do you think the following SQL query answers?SELECT Major, COUNT(*)
FROM Student
GROUP BY Major;

In this chapter, you learned that a database is an organized collection of related data. Databases are used across organizations to store, manage, and retrieve data efficiently.

You also learned that:

Data are raw facts.

Information is data made meaningful.

A DBMS manages databases.

Relational databases organize data into tables.

Tables contain rows and columns.

SQL is used to communicate with relational databases.

In the next chapter, we will begin looking more closely at how data is modeled before it becomes a database.

Export to Binder or Google Colab

records.content:
As you can see you can also export these notebooks to Binder or Google Colab.

This will take you to their respective websites but you can work with them there, if you would like!
I know it tradition to have the refences at the end of books, but when you are standing on the shoulders of giants. You thank them first.

Also, a huge thank you to Adam Lavely (

https://​github​.com​/adamlavely) for developing some of the intial notebooks!

Jupyter is a powerful suite of tools that allows us to do many things.

Jupyter is capable of running Julia, Python and R, as well as some other things.
Each box is called a cell.
Text Cells allow you to add text (via Markdown), which includes tables, images, links, bullet lists, numbered lists, LaTeX, blockquote, among other things.

Cells can be run using the Run button ► or selecting one of the run options under the Run menu.

Try this out! You can change what is in the cell and rerun the same cell, which is useful for debugging.

2 + 2

In a new cell, figure out what 5315 + 5618 is.

5315 + 5618 + 2

When using other languages in Jupyter/JupyterLab, we need to designate the cell we are using with this langauge or function. To “activate” these language, we need to use ✨ MAGIC ✨. Here are a couple of examples.

%%html
  1. apple
  2. banana
  3. cookies

%%svg
<svg height="100" width="100">
  <circle cx="50" cy="50" r="20" stroke="black" stroke-width="3" fill="steelblue" />
</svg>

%%capture
!pip install ipython-sql



%load_ext sql



%sql sqlite:///exampleDatabase.db



More information can be found here: 

Jupyter ✨ MAGIC ✨

hierarchy.lvl1: Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab

records.content:
In this example, we will start using SQL in Google Colab Notebook.

But first, let’s just review some basics of Google Colab.

Let’s start with just running a bit of “code.” The cell I am currently is a “Text” cell (using +Text). For code, we want to use a “code” cell (+Code). The below statement will just add two numbers together, nothing too fancy.

2 + 2



Your turn!

Click on the “+Code” button in the top-left corner.

Figure out the answer for the following math problem.(102939129381 / 231)  + 12300

Pretty easy so far, huh?

OK, next to the SQL.

hierarchy.lvl1: Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab

records.content:
One of the cool tricks you can do in Jupyter Notebooks is something so amazing, you might even think its magic! Well, it technically is.

Jupyter has a number of built in utilities, which they call 

magic. Don’t worry about trying to learn them all, and we only need one specific type of magic. SQL magic. Actually, we technically need %load_ext magic, but this will load SQL for us.

#%%capture
#!pip install ipython-sql sqlalchemy



%load_ext sql



%config SqlMagic.style = '_DEPRECATED_DEFAULT'



This is a built-in Python SQL toolkit provide by (another magic reference) 

SQL Alchemy. Again, you don’t need to worry too much about SQL Alchemy, only that we can now use SQL code in our notebook.

The next step is to create a database, so in this case, we use the following command. Note: we will be expanding on this throughout the semester, but for now... it creates a single, SQLite database for this notebook. Also, if the command runs successfully, you should see " ‘Connected: 

studentDatabase.db’ "

%sql sqlite:///studentDatabase.db



This is your database for adding tables and inserting data, BUT this is temporary. So, do not assume that this will save between sessions. If you want to save this database for later, go to the left (where the folder is), select the folder, and then download the database (right-click on the database).

###Types of SQL Interactions

Here is a list of common interactions with database.

Now it is time to create our first table. This is where the SQL commands become available to us. The first command is...

hierarchy.lvl1: Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab

records.content:
Create will create a table. In this case, we will call the table Student, and we will need to tell the table the names of the attributes and what type of attribute they are as well. For now, we will be using only 3 types:

INTEGER - whole numbers (example: 3)

REAL - floats or real numbers (example: 3.14)

TEXT - text information (example: “hello world!”)

For us, we need 6 attributes, all of which will be text.

Let’s create 1 table for student information.

%%sql
DROP TABLE IF EXISTS Student;
CREATE TABLE Student(PSUID text primary key, firstName text, lastName text, streetAddress text, city text, state text, gpa real);





%sql SELECT * from Student;






Now let’s add some data to this table. In this case we use...

hierarchy.lvl1: Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab

records.content:
SELECT allows us to select all or part of the data. For now, all we want to do is get back from the table all the data in the table. For this we will use:

SELECT  (wildcard!) FROM table name

The wildcard basically means All the Attributes in the table

We can also limit the number of tuples returned.

%%sql
SELECT * FROM Student;





%%sql
SELECT * FROM Student;





Other things we can do with SELECT.

Let’s say we want to know which database we are connected to and what tables we have created. We would use the following SQL.

%sql SELECT name FROM sqlite_master;

hierarchy.lvl1: Start learning SQL in Google Colab
Start learning SQL in Google Colab

records.content:
Great! We created a table and put some data into the table, but how do we... destroy it?

DROP is used to “delete” the table and all tuples.

** WARNING WARNING **

A table that is dropped is completely deleted, including ALL DATA. Be careful with this.

%%sql
#DROP TABLE Student;



So, now we check all the tables we have...

%sql SELECT name FROM sqlite_master;
